step.actionDisplay: RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 16 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions (first 10): RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 87, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Player at cols 1-3, rows 32-35. White strip at col 0. Left gray box at rows 28-31, cols 16-19. Right gray box grew to 8 rows. Used 70/71 actions - very inefficient so far.
- Plan: Move RIGHT to position below left gray box, drop strip to grow it. Need to be more efficient.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 86 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: OK, I've moved RIGHT 16 times. Let me check where the player is now and whether they're below the left gray box.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("Current nodes:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['N', 'W', 'c', 'b', 'g']:
        print(f"  {n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The player is at cols 60-62, rows 32-35. The white strip is at col 63, rows 32-35. So the player moved past the left gray box (cols 16-19) and all the way to the right edge (col 60-62).
I've been going back and forth without making progress. Let me step back and think about what's happening.
The key observation is: when I dropped the white strip below the right gray box, the box grew. But I've been moving the player around without dropping the strip below the left or center boxes.
Now the player is at the right edge. I need to:
1. Move LEFT to position below the left gray box (cols 16-19)
2. Drop the white strip (SPACE) to grow the left gray box
3. Then move to position below the center bar and drop again
But I keep overshooting. Let me be more careful about positioning.
Player is at cols 60-62. Left gray box is at cols 16-19. I need to move LEFT about 41 columns to get to col 17 (center of left gray box).
But earlier when I tried moving LEFT 33 columns, it stopped after 15 moves (hit the left edge). That was because the player was at cols 52-54 and moved LEFT 15 times to reach col 1.
Wait, that doesn't make sense. If the player was at cols 52-54 and moved LEFT 15 times, they should be at cols 37-39, not col 1. Unless the player moves 3 columns per LEFT action (since the player is 3 columns wide).
Let me check: player is 3 columns wide (cols 60-62). Each LEFT move shifts the player by 1 column. So 16 RIGHT moves from cols 1-3 would put the player at cols 17-19. But the player ended up at cols 60-62. That's 57 columns right, not 16.
Wait, that doesn't add up. Let me reconsider. The player started at cols 1-3. After 16 RIGHT moves, the player is at cols 60-62. That's 57 columns moved in 16 steps. That's about 3.5 columns per step.
Actually, I think the player moves 3 columns per step (matching their width). So 16 * 3 = 48 columns. Starting from col 1, that's col 49. But the player is at col 60. Hmm, that doesn't match either.
Wait, let me reconsider. Earlier the player was at cols 52-54. After moving LEFT 15 times, the player was at cols 1-3. That's 51 columns in 15 steps, or 3.4 columns per step. And from cols 1-3, after 16 RIGHT moves, the player is at cols 60-62. That's 57 columns in 16 steps, or 3.56 columns per step.
I think the player moves 3 columns per step (their width). So:
- From cols 52-54, LEFT 15 times: 52 - 15*3 = 52 - 45 = 7. But the player ended at col 1. So maybe it's 15 * 3.4 = 51, and 52 - 51 = 1. Close enough.
Actually, I think the player moves by their full width (3 columns) plus 1 column of gap each step. So each step is 4 columns. 15 * 4 = 60, and 52 - 60 = -8, which doesn't work.
Let me just check the actual movement: from cols 1-3 to cols 60-62 in 16 steps. That's 57 columns / 16 steps = 3.56 columns per step. This is not a clean integer.
Actually, I think the player is 3 columns wide and moves 3 columns per step. So from col 1 to col 60 is 59 columns, which is 59/3 = 19.67 steps. But we only did 16 steps. So maybe the player moves faster than 3 columns per step.
OK, I'm overthinking this. Let me just figure out where the player needs to be and move there.
The left gray box is at cols 16-19. The player needs to be at rows 32-35 (which they already are) and cols 16-19 (or close).
Currently the player is at cols 60-62. I need to move LEFT to get to cols 16-19. That's about 41-44 columns left.
If each LEFT move is about 3-4 columns, I need about 10-14 LEFT moves.
But I keep overshooting. Let me try a smaller number, like 10 LEFT moves, and check the result.
Actually, wait. I just realized something. The white strip is at col 63, which is the right edge. When I move LEFT, the white strip follows on the right side (trailing edge). So the strip will be behind the player.
Let me try moving LEFT 12 times and check the position.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Player at cols 60-62, rows 32-35. White strip at col 63. Left gray box at rows 28-31, cols 16-19. I keep overshooting when moving. Need to be more careful.
Plan: Move LEFT ~12 steps to position below left gray box, check position, then drop strip.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move LEFT to position below left gray box
# Player at cols 60-62, need cols ~17-19
# Distance: ~41-43 columns
# Each step seems to be ~3-4 columns
# Try 12 steps first
action(['LEFT'] * 12)
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
